data.title: Higher Long-Term Interest Rates If Trump Fires Powell From The Fed
data.description:
President Trump has been unhappy with the Federal Reserve’s interest rate decisions, which in 2025 have left policy rates unchanged. The president, along with his fellow real estate developers, prefers low interest rates. But the Federal Reserve Act protects the Fed chair’s removal except "for cause." The president may have cause in connection with building renovations at the Fed.

Chair Jerome Powell’s removal would enable the president to appoint a successor, most likely someone inclined to lower the Fed’s policy rate, called the Federal Funds rate or Fed Funds. Long-term interest rates, however, are set by financial markets and are sensitive to inflation expectations. And easier monetary policy would likely raise inflation expectations, raising bond and mortgage interest rates even as short-term interest rates fall.

President Trump’s animosity toward Powell has been public for a while. The law allows the president to replace the chair “for cause,” a phrase not defined in the act governing the Fed. The White House’s Office of Management and Budget director, Russell Vought, has sent Powell a challenging letter alleging mismanagement of the $2.5 billion building renovation. In common employment cases, courts generally put the burden of proof on the employer. However, the constitution grants the president authority to manage the executive branch as he sees fit, though subject to the law. At this point, it’s hard to know how the case would play out in a court of law.

Bond markets would most likely take a Powell firing as negative. Stock markets might like it, though that prediction is much more speculative.

Long-term bond interest rates reflect inflation expectations. The economist Irving Fisher described interest rates as the sum of the “real” interest rate plus expected inflation. Bond holders will be repaid in dollars (or whatever currency the bond is issued in). If the purchasing power of a dollar is falling, the interest rate must be higher to make bond holders whole. And the bond issuers will be willing to pay a higher interest rate, knowing that when the time comes to pay off the bond, they will be paying with dollars of lower purchasing power.

Common economic models predict that persistently lower interest rates will lead to more inflation. Lower rates would not be very inflationary in an economy with substantial slack, but currently unemployment is low and businesses have little excess capacity. With a new chair, short-term interest rates might fall - that’s the Fed’s mechanism for monetary policy - but the Fed cannot stop financial markets from repricing long-term interest rates.

Not only will Powell’s ouster raise long-term interest rates due to higher expected inflation, it may also diminish the value of American assets to international investors. The United States has been a large, relatively stable, low-inflation economy. Investors like that. But Powell’s ouster may also lead to expectations of more economic volatility: more booms and more busts.

The strongest argument for low inflation focuses on economic stability. Higher-inflation economies tend to have periodic anti-inflation monetary policies, which lead often to recessions, then followed by stimulative policies to get people employed again - which eventually lead to more inflation. Back in the late 1979-82 era, Fed chair Paul Volcker committed to bringing inflation down and keeping it down. There followed an amazingly long period with few recessions. Long-term interest rates gradually declined. The 10-year Treasury bond hit a high of 15% in the midst of the anti-inflation program in 1981. It dropped below 10% in the late 1980s, then kept falling. Before the pandemic, it had dropped below two percent. That was due to both expected inflation being low and economic stability expected to continue.

It's not baked in the cake that a Trump appointment of the Fed chair will immediately ease interest rates. Monetary policy is actually made by the Federal Open Market Committee. This group consists of the seven presidentially appointed governors (who serve long terms in office) plus four of the 12 regional Federal Reserve Bank presidents (who are appointed by their respective boards of directors). The other eight presidents participate in discussions but do not vote. The chair has power only to the extent that he or she can persuade the other committee members to go along. In the case of a push for lower interest rates coming from the White House, some committee members may disagree with the policy, and others may object to being pressured.

Yet, even if the FOMC does not do President Trump’s bidding, the global investment community may feel that White House pressure will eventually come. Thus, the prediction of higher long-term interest rates seems pretty certain if Trump dismisses Powell.

Stock prices present a greater forecasting challenge. Stock investors like lower short-term interest rates. That lowers current financing costs and is likely to provide short-term stimulus to the economy. But investors also dislike high long-term interest rates and economic volatility. Political chaos doesn't seem to be much of a detriment, at least on the evidence from the first six months of Trump’s second term of office.

Business leaders should probably stick to a base case for planning of continuation of current monetary policy. Those especially sensitive to long-term interest rates, such as real estate and construction, will want to develop bad-news contingency plans for higher interest rates. Some companies will benefit from lower short-term interest rates (corporate finance, auto sales). They need good-news contingency plans to take advantage of the possible cuts in short-term interest rates. And all companies should develop general contingency plans for a more volatile, boom/bust economy. Let’s hope that like insurance, those plans are never used.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Welltower And Realty Income: Time For A REIT Rotation
data.description:
Today's theme is sector rotation! Sector rotation is a strategy where investors move portfolio positions between different sectors based on the stage of the economic lifecycle. The idea is that some sectors tend to outperform while others underperform with a degree of regularity. By rotating their investments into underperforming sectors expected to rise and out of outperforming sectors expected to fall, you can spin a wheel of constant appreciation…in theory.

This strategy applies broadly across the market to a variety of asset classes. In fact, it's been a tried and true investment strategy for generations, proving to serve investors a fortuitous cycle. Over time, sector rotation has become less effective as transparent and market efficiency improve over years and years. Despite challenges, the strategy remains broadly successful, especially in niche asset classes, such as real estate.

It's no secret that REITs sit at the core of REITer's Digest. After all, it's the lion's share of my professional experience, so it's to be expected that REITs will come up frequently in my publications. Today will be no different as we explore sector rotation in commercial real estate.

Setting The Stage

Sector rotation as a broad strategy involves capitalizing on market exuberance and upswings as well as downturns. Asset classes go in and out of style in commercial real estate, but by and large, their staying power has remained through generations.

What do I mean?

Look at the big three: office, multifamily, and industrial. Each has had its peaks and valleys over long time horizons. Investors who were willing to sell at the peaks and confidently buy in the valleys have historically been rewarded. This strategy is effective. Even better, the trends are often relatively easy to identify.

In today's discussion, I will present an opportunity for sector rotation in real estate. We will take in incisive look into two of the top REITs in their respective sectors. While one company is currently trading at all-time highs, riding the momentum of key tailwinds, the other offers new value as renewed interest returns to net lease. I float the idea of selling one to buy the other based on valuation discrepancies in a thematic play as sectors begin to rotate.

Welltower (NYSE:WELL), the world's largest publicly traded senior living REIT, has surged over the past twelve months to all-time highs on account of market confidence and emerging demand drivers.

In contrast, Realty Income (NYSE:O), the blue chip of the net lease sector, remains in the doldrums following several punishing years that have dramatically impacted the company from the standpoint of operating challenges and opportunity cost.

While investors hold the status quo, I see emerging market dynamics that could suggest getting ahead of the game could be a wise decision.

The Sell: Welltower

Over the past several years, senior living demand has expanded significantly. The pandemic uncovered flaws and issues within senior living that cause market upheaval. Following several years of aging demographics and limited new deliveries coming online, there is once again a supply-demand mismatch in the marketplace. For WELL, the world's largest senior living REIT, this has materialized into revenue growth and increased demand.

According to Cushman & Wakefield, senior living fundamentals are strong and getting stronger.

  • U.S. senior living property market fundamentals continue to strengthen. Stabilized occupancy trended upward for the seventeenth consecutive quarter, surpassing 89% overall, with secondary markets reaching 90% occupancy, a level not obtained since 2017.
  • The number of occupied units reached a new all-time-high in Q1 2025 with net absorption outpacing supply growth by 2.5 to 1, as inventory growth remains near historic lows.
  • Annual rent growth, though tapered from prior quarters, has remained intact, averaging 3.9% in Q1 2025, a dip that is likely seasonal as the sector emerges from winter months. This outsized occupancy and rent growth has helped counterbalance short-term turbulence in the broader capital markets.

The market has taken note, and the company is currently trading at an extraordinary valuation from both an earnings multiple and share yield perspective. What was once a high yielding REIT is now considered a growth powerhouse by Mr. Market. Shares of WELL have more than doubled over the past three years as the company's earnings multiple balloon past 30x.

The question becomes if the company is able to deliver on the lofty expectations. Often in a slow-moving game like real estate, the answer is no.

In fact, it appears as though trouble is brewing in senior living. Just recently, one of WELL's tenants declared bankruptcy as mounting expenses in the industry overtook their revenue generating abilities. Historically, WELL has been able to navigate tenant issues including limiting ties with Genesis Healthcare ahead of their bankruptcy.

In 2021, Welltower announced it was cutting most of its ties with Genesis in a massive $880 million deal, which included terminating leases with the nursing home giant on 51 of its properties.

Even still, according to Bisnow, Genesis owes WELL a significant amount of money.

Genesis owes roughly $700M to secured creditors, including Welltower, a REIT with a $100B market cap, and Omega Healthcare Investors, another REIT it leases properties from, according to the WSJ.

Interestingly enough, this was identified as a key consideration in Hindenburg Research's short report on WELL published several years ago.

  • Red flags indicate that another of Welltower's distressed deals, a 2021 restructuring with its troubled 4th largest operator, Genesis Healthcare, mirrored the latest 'miracle' deal.
  • In late 2021, evidence suggests that Welltower quietly disposed of 21 of its distressed Genesis assets to a Gefner-affiliated firm, once again handing skilled nursing facilities over to an inexperienced manager and clearing its books of the mess.

The issue with WELL lies in the company's current valuation. While tailwinds continue to power the company ahead, trading at more than 30x FFO tells a cautionary tale for a senior living REIT which has historically been a high yielder. While WELL is priced to perfection, I am beginning to doubt the feasibility or likelihood that the market will deliver a senior living renaissance over the next couple of years. Considering shares of WELL are up around 50% over the past twelve months, taking profits looks appealing.

The question becomes where to put them.

The Buy: Realty Income

While O has established an impressive, generational track record, the company is facing a flat five year period. Shares appear to be stuck as the company faces increasing risks and a risk free rate that is challenging the value of the company's monthly dividend.

Shares have more or less been flat over the past seven years despite the company continuing to grow earnings per share and the dividend. While O's current dividend and AFFO yields are historically high, their attractiveness is limited when compared to a ten year treasury yielding 4.5%.

However, if rates are to decline, O presents a great opportunity. That said, the "if" in that sentence is powerful considering how long we have waited for some relief in rates. With that said, the tide appears to be turning.

Blackstone (BX) is making a considerable push into the net lease sector as long duration REITs become increasingly attractive. The massive alternative asset manager has been packing on net lease assets. Most surprisingly was the announcement that net lease real estate would become a material component of BX's mortgage REIT, the Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT), as the fund struggles to redeploy repaid loans into a complicated commercial mortgage market. I suppose if you close your eyes and use your imagination, one could make the argument that net leased assets are a real estate credit investment.

"As we look ahead, we are leveraging the same Blackstone platform advantages and entrepreneurial DNA to look across the real estate credit universe and identify the best-suited incremental, strategic opportunities for our business," Katie Keenan, CEO of Blackstone Mortgage, said on the call. "With interest rates remaining elevated, a positive outlook for the U.S., consumer and essential needs-based retail showing resilient performance, we see a compelling setup today to build a credit-oriented, diversified net lease strategy."

The important takeaways here is that the smart money begins to move towards long leased assets, it could be a leading indicator that interest could soon return to net lease. For O, the world's largest net lease REIT and the de facto net lease index fund, this could become a thematic play.

While investors wait, the company's nearly 6% yield is ample to compensate us while we wait for the market to once again love the Big O. Better yet, investing today means buying in at 13.3x forward adjusted funds from operations per share. This is a far more reasonable earnings multiple than the >20x AFFO where O was priced several years ago. For a company with a solid balance sheet and a continued track record of dividend growth, this multiple is considerably more attractive and leaves plenty of room for future growth.

O does not come without certain risks and challenges. Over the past year, I've published several critical articles of O mainly discussing their issue with Mission Creep, driving their attention away from their bread and butter business.

My concern around O lies towards the future. As we illustrated, the company's growth has slowed over the past decade. Traditional growth drivers like acquisitions are no longer moving the needle as a percentage of enterprise value. This means changing share level metrics will also become more difficult as larger transactions become necessary.

Since I published that article in July, Realty Income has continued to expand and adapt their business including the addition of a private capital platform to generate asset management fees. While I see downsides, I must acknowledge their creativity and ingenuity. In the meantime, the longest running dividend in net lease continues to grow.

Key Risks

As with any investment strategy, there are risks that need to be considered. In this case, there are two categories of risk that should be assessed: strategic risks and opportunity cost.

In the first case, sector rotation carries key risks. The strategy involves timing the market by identifying low points and high points, which means trying to predict future trends. While using valuations as a guiding tool for identifying bubble-like situations across the market is helpful, it is not perfect. For example, people have been calling out tech as being due for a sector rotation for the past decade. Instead, the largest names have bucked the trend by delivering on their stratospheric growth estimates. Specific to the aforementioned trade, WELL could continue to deliver on peaking growth estimates and similarly, O could fail to make a timely turnaround. This possibility leads to the next risk which is opportunity cost.

Assuming the prediction of a slowdown in senior living and revival of net lease are correct, there are additional risk factors. For example, switching between two specific names, such as WELL and O, presents opportunity cost risk as other sectors or net lease REITs could outperform O. Going one step further, trading into a specific net lease REIT also introduces a layer of risk at a company level. As I have outlined before, O's business is quickly changing leaving a degree of execution risk. This could be mitigated by opting for an ETF targeting the net lease sector, like the Fundamental Income Net Lease Real Estate ETF (NETL).

Either risk would diminish the effectiveness of the proposed swap, even if the thematic predictions around each industry are correct. However, instead of focusing on making the perfect trade, there is value in identifying two sectors that are ready to rotate.

Key Takeaways

This article was intended to highlight two REITs that are of similar quality, but currently sitting on two ends of the sentiment spectrum. While senior living demand skyrockets, driving the multiple of WELL to an extreme level, net lease remains largely in the penalty box. However, eyes are beginning to turn towards assets with long leases and creditworthy tenants. Sitting in that chair, I see a ripe opportunity for sector rotation between two of the largest real estate companies. Trading WELL for O means a considerable change in valuation and dividend cash flow to the shareholder.

As illustrated above, shares of O are yielding more than twice shares of WELL, while enjoying a similar credit rating. In fact, I would argue that O's balance sheet is more conservative and likely better positioned for the next three years than the expansive, growing WELL. I continue to emphasize the importance of valuation as a factor of shareholder return. In fact, I would call it the single most important piece of the puzzle. Time for a REIT rotation!

data.title: Precious Metals Royalty And Streaming Companies - June 2025 Report
data.description:
Precious metals royalty and streaming companies represent a very interesting sub-industry of the precious metals mining industry. They provide some leverage to the growing metals prices, similar to the typical mining companies; however, they are less risky in comparison to them. Their incomes are derived from royalty and streaming agreements. Under a metal streaming agreement, the streaming company provides an upfront payment to acquire the right to future deliveries of a predefined percentage of the metal production of a mining operation.

The streaming company also pays some ongoing payments that are usually well below the market price of the metal. They can be set as a fixed sum (e.g., $300/toz gold) or as a percentage (e.g., 20% of the prevailing gold price), or a combination of both (e.g., the lower of a) $300/toz gold and b) 20% of the prevailing gold price). The royalties usually apply to a small fraction of the mining project production (usually 1-3%), and they are not connected with ongoing payments. They can have various forms, but the most common is a small percentage of the net smelter return ("NSR"). The NSR is calculated as revenues from the sale of the mined products minus transportation and refining costs.

To better track the overall performance of the whole sub-industry, I created a capitalization-weighted index (the Precious Metals Royalty and Streaming Index) consisting of 11 companies (in June 2020, expanded to 15). Later, based on the inquiries of readers, I also introduced an equal-weighted version of the index. Until March 2021, both indices included the same companies and were calculated back to January 2019.

However, some major changes occurred in April 2021. Due to the boom of the royalty and streaming industry and the emergence of many new companies, the indices experienced two major changes. First of all, the market capitalization-weighted index was modified to include only the 5 biggest companies: Franco-Nevada (FNV), Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), Royal Gold (RGLD), OR Royalties (OR), and Sandstorm Gold (SAND). The combined weight of these 5 companies on the old index was around 95%, therefore, the small companies had only a negligible impact on their performance. The values of the index were recalculated back to January 2019, and between January 2019 and March 2021, the difference in the overall performance of the old and the new index was only 2.29 percentage points. The second change is related to the equally weighted index that was expanded to 20 companies.

The previous editions of the monthly report can be found here: May 2019, June 2019, July 2019, August 2019, September 2019, October 2019, November 2019, December 2019, January 2020, February 2020, March 2020, April 2020, May 2020, June 2020, July 2020, August 2020, September 2020, October 2020, November 2020, December 2020, January 2021, February 2021, March 2021, April 2021, May 2021, June 2021, July 2021, August 2021, September 2021, October 2021, November 2021, December 2021, January 2022, February 2022, March 2022, April 2022, May 2022, June 2022, July 2022, August 2022, September 2022, October 2022, November 2022, December 2022, January 2023, February 2023, March 2023, April 2023, May 2023, June 2023, July 2023, August 2023, September 2023, October 2023, November 2023, December 2023, January 2024, February 2024, March 2024, April 2024, May 2024, June 2024, July 2024, August 2024, September 2024, October 2024, November 2024, December 2024, January 2025, February 2025, March 2025, April 2025, May 2025.

Wheaton Precious Metals remains the biggest precious metals R&S company by market capitalization. In June, it even exceeded the $40 billion level for the first time in its history. Second-placed Franco-Nevada lags significantly, with a market capitalization of less than $32 billion. No major changes occurred in the ranking, only Elemental Altus Royalties (OTCQX:ELEMF) outgrew Orogen Royalties (OGNRF), and at the very bottom, Star Royalties (OTCQX:STRFF) outgrew Silver Crown Royalties (SCRI:CA). Therefore, Silver Crown ended June as the smallest of the companies, with a market capitalization of slightly less than $13 million.

In general, June was a good month for the precious metals R&S companies. Some of them recorded impressive double-digit gains. On the other hand, five of them, including Franco-Nevada and Royal Gold, ended in red numbers. The highest gains were recorded by Star Royalties. Its more than 30% share price growth was fueled by positive news related to its Copperstone gold stream, moreover, the share price needed some correction, following the steep two-months long bearish trend. More than 30% growth experienced also Elemental. It was fueled by the announcement that Tether Investments became a major shareholder, with an option to increase its stake in the company to more than 50%. On the other hand, the biggest loss (-10.73%) experienced shares of Vox Royalty (VOXR). However, it is hard to connect the decline with a specific news.

In June, silver outperformed gold significantly. As a result, the share price of the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) grew by 9.37%, while the share price of the SPDR Gold Trust ETF (GLD) grew only by 0.41%. This helped the share price of the VanEck Vectors Gold Miners ETF (GDX) and the VanEck Vectors Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ) to increase by 2.78% and 3.48% respectively. The precious metals R&S indices provided some mixed results. The Precious Metals R&S Index grew only by 0.67% due to the weak performance of Royal Gold and Franco-Nevada. On the other hand, the value of the Precious Metals R&S Equally Weighted Index, where the smaller companies have much stronger weight, increased by 6.76%.

The June News

The news flow was relatively weak in June. No major deals took place. However, some companies came up with encouraging portfolio updates.

Franco-Nevada (FNV) announced that following engagement with the Government of Panama's legal counsel, it has agreed to suspend the arbitration proceedings related to the Cobre Panama mine.

Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) announced some senior management changes. The company was also named among Corporate Knights' 50 Best Corporate Citizens in Canada.

OR Royalties (OR) announced a $13 million acquisition of a 100% silver stream on Orla Mining's (ORLA) South Railroad project. The feasibility for the project should be completed this year, the permits are expected by the summer of 2026, with production expected to commence before the end of 2027. OR will be entitled to purchase 100% of silver produced at the mine, at ongoing payments equal to 15% of the prevailing silver price. The company also reported new developments at several assets included in its portfolio.

On June 9, OR announced an amendment to its revolving credit facility. The original C$550 million credit facility with an uncommitted accordion of up to C$200 million was converted into a $650 million credit facility with an uncommitted accordion of $200 million. The facility will mature on May 30, 2029, and it will bear an interest of SOFR or CORPA plus 1.45-2.75% p.a.

Sandstorm Gold (SAND) provided some portfolio news. The main news is that First Majestic Silver (AG) made a significant gold-silver discovery at the Santa Elena mine. The new Santo Nino vein has been traced over 1 km along strike, and 400 meters down-dip. Sandstorm owns a 20% gold stream that entitles it to gold purchases at ongoing payments of $487/toz gold. At the same time, Lundin Gold (OTCQX:LUGDF) has expanded the drill program at the Fruta del Norte mine to 108,000 metres. Some of the recent drill results include 72.8 g/t gold over 7.95 meters, or 40.6 g/t gold over 13.9 meters. Sandstorm owns a 0.9% NSR royalty on Fruta del Norte. And some great drill results also arrived from Entree Resources' (OTCQB:ERLFF) Hugo North Extension which is a part of the Oyu Tolgoi mine. Entree drilled 260 meters grading 3.29% copper and 2.08 g/t gold. Sandstorm owns a 0.33% copper and 4.47% gold and silver stream on Hugo North Extension, with ongoing payments of $0.5/lb copper, $220/toz gold, and $5/toz silver.

The company also declared the quarterly dividend. It will pay C$0.02 ($0.015) per share on July 25. Shareholders of record as of July 15 will be entitled to this payment.

Metalla Royalty & Streaming (MTA) announced a $40 million revolving credit facility with a $35 million accordion provided by the Bank of Montreal and National Bank Financial. The facility bears an interest of SOFR plus 2.5-3.5% p.a. At the same time, Metalla fully repaid its old convertible loan facility ($13.1 million). The company also released its 2025 Asset Handbook.

EMX Royalty (EMX) sold its Nordic generative enterprise for $335,000 to First Nordic Metals Corporation. EMX will also be entitled to a 1% NSR royalty on any newly generated projects in Sweden and Finland during the next five years.

Vox Royalty (VOXR) announced the receipt of Writ of Summons and Statement of Claim related to its September 2023 purchase of the Red Hill royalty. The plaintiff claims that he had the right of first refusal, which was breached.

The company also provided a portfolio update. The most important news is that earthworks, including box-cut excavation, have started at the Sulphur Springs copper-zinc-lead-silver project. An updated feasibility study for this project is expected by the end of this year. Vox holds an A$2/tonne production royalty capped at A$3.7 million, and A$0.8/tonne uncapped production royalty over the Kangaroo Caves deposit, a part of the Sulphur Springs project. Another positive news is that the permitting of the Bowdens silver mine is progressing (Vox holds a 0.85% Gross Revenue Royalty over the property).

On June 23, Vox announced that the Horseshoe Lights project is being fast-tracked to production, which could start before the end of this year. Vox holds a 3% NSR royalty over Horseshoe Lights.

As of June 30, Vox Royalty was added to the Russell 2000 and Russell 3000 stock indices.

Sailfish Royalty (OTCQX:SROYF) announced that the U.S. Bureau of Land Management issued the Final Environmental Impact Statement for the Spring Valley gold mine. The Record of Decision is expected by August 11, and the mine construction may start immediately thereafter, with the first gold production in 2028. Sailfish holds a 3% NSR royalty that covers the majority of the proposed open pit, with the remainder of the property covered by a 1.5% and a 0.5% NSR royalty respectively.

Orogen Royalties (OGNRF) reported Q1 financial results. The company generated revenues of $1.4 million, operating cash flow of $0.8 million, and net income of $0.8 million. Unfortunately, Orogen's cash flows remain fully dependent on the Ermitano royalty. As of the end of Q1, Orogen held cash of $18.3 million, and no debt.

On June 27, Orogen announced that its shareholders approved the acquisition of Orogen by Triple Flag Precious Metals (TFPM), announced in April.

Elemental Altus Royalties (OTCQX:ELEMF) noted that Tether Investments has acquired 78,421,780 shares of Elemental from La Mancha Investments, at a price of C$1.55 per share. Tether owns 37.8% of Elemental now. Tether also has an option to increase its stake to 51.8% by acquiring shares held by Alpha 1 SPV.

Empress Royalty (OTCQX:EMPYF) granted 1.4 million stock options to purchase 1.4 million shares at a price of $0.68, 1.75 million restricted share units, and 650,000 deferred share units, to certain directors, officers, employees, and consultants of the company.

Star Royalties (OTCQX:STRFF) provided an update regarding the 4% gold stream from Minera Alamos' (OTCQX:MAIFF) Copperstone gold mine. An updated technical study is expected in Q4, and the mine should be fast-tracked to restart production next year.

Silver Crown Royalties (SCRI:CA) announced the acquisition of a 4.5% royalty on silver produced at Kuya Silver's (OTCQB:KUYAF) Bethania silver mine, for $3 million in cash and $2 million in units (each unit consists of 1 share and 0.5 warrants with a strike price of C$13 and 3-year maturity). Silver Crown should receive 4,500 toz silver per quarter over the first 4 quarters, 9,000 toz silver per quarter over the next 4 quarters, and 12,375 toz silver per quarter over the remainder of the mine life. After 475,000 toz silver is delivered, the royalty will be reduced to 1%. The royalty will provide immediate cash flow.

On June 19, Silver Crown announced the closing of the first tranche of its non-brokered offering of units, worth $2 million.

The July Outlook

The month of July began with gold moving sideways, however, the silver price experienced a breakout and seems to be primed for the psychological barrier of $40/toz. The precious metals R&S companies haven't experienced major moves by now. With the exception of Royal Gold, whose share price dipped by more than 10% following the announcement of the Sandstorm Gold acquisition. For now, it looks like the overall impression of July will be mixed. Unless the gold price decides to make a more significant move to the upside or to the downside. The silver price seems to be ignored. Even Silver Crown has moved only slightly up so far.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PEO: Energy And Materials Equities CEF (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

We have not covered the Adams Natural Resources Fund (NYSE:PEO) for almost two years now. Our last article was published with a 'Sell' rating, and we argued that investors should simply use ETF building blocks to achieve the same results as PEO, but with a lower expense ratio.

With inflation rearing its ugly head again, we are going to revisit PEO, its performance and analytics, and frame this CEF in today's macro environment.

Let's see first if there are any notable portfolio changes

Firstly let us look at the industry and sub-industry breakdowns in PEO:

  • Integrated Oil & Gas: 36.4%
  • E&P: 21.4%
  • Storage & Transportation: 10.9%
  • Refining: 6.2%
  • Equipment & Services: 5.9%
  • Chemicals: 12.7%
  • Metals & Mining: 2.8%
  • Containers & Packaging: 1.8%
  • Construction materials: 1.3%

Since our last review the Energy section of the portfolio expanded a bit, with Materials now down slightly to roughly 19%. The largest names in the portfolio are still quite similar, being the top names one can find in the Materials Select Sector ETF (XLB) and the Energy Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLE).

The CEF therefore continues to be a blend of mainly energy names with a large materials sleeve. We grant the fact that the name has been around for a very long time, but there are a few new funds in the market that try to capture the same dynamic, while others are layering in new risk factors to optimize long term returns. Let us have a look.

Performance

Since we last covered this name, let us see how the CEF did versus a simple portfolio blend and some newcomers in the space:

We are using a total return figure here in order to incorporate the fund dividends and normalize returns across CEF and ETF asset classes. PEO has done well, with a 17% total return, a figure that shines when compared to a newcomer in the space, namely the FlexShares Morningstar Upstream Natural Resources Fund (GUNR). GUNR is up only +10% versus +17% for PEO during the same time-frame.

Let us now go back to that simple portfolio composed of 20% XLB and 80% XLE: that portfolio would have been up +26% in the same timeframe, thus consolidating our prior analysis. In our prior article we assigned the CEF a 'Sell' not on a net basis (i.e., expecting a negative return), but on a comparative basis with a simple XLE/XLB portfolio. Turns out that simple works better.

Premium/Discount to NAV

The CEF has historically had a large discount to NAV:

That figure has contracted severely since the beginning of 2024, bringing the CEF to a -9% discount to NAV, a figure which is the top of the range for this name. Can the CEF go even higher? Yes. Does it need a tangible driver? You bet. The fund needs an outperformance in energy names to continue to tighten its discount to NAV or some aggressive corporate actions in the form of share repurchases.

Distributions are covered

What we like about this name is the fact that it does not overdistribute:

The fund does not try to lure in investors via very high distribution rates funded by return of capital, but passes on what it makes. As an investor you are getting here the long term returns of energy and materials equities. With a fairly stable NAV over time and an inexistent leverage ratio (sub 1%), the name uses the CEF structure to distill capital gains into dividends.

Inflationary pressures have a new escape valve

While historically energy and materials have been good sectors to ride inflation, the new macro-world has seen the emergence of gold and bitcoin as escape valves for capital preservation. In the past three years we have seen a parabolic move in the last two asset classes:

With the emergence of these two assets we have also seen tweaks in respect to inflation hedging funds, and we recently put an article forward which covers the VanEck Real Assets ETF (RAAX), which contains energy, materials but also gold and gold miners.

There are various reasons investors buy a fund like PEO, but given its structure (i.e. no leverage), the name represents a structural means of extracting dividends from a portfolio of energy and materials equities. If you are considering PEO for inflation hedging, also keep in mind that gold and crypto have seen a massive bid as of late.

Why an upgrade?

With inflation stickier than expected, we are of the opinion PEO will continue to produce robust net positive returns, although smaller than a simple XLE/XLB portfolio. Net positive returns, however, cannot be ignored, and an outperformance in the underlying holdings might even see the discount narrow further. In our mind these are factors to upgrade the CEF to 'Hold' when compared to our last rating. While savvy investors can indeed build their own portfolio using cheap simple ETF building blocks, some retail investors prefer the one-stop-shop nature of PEO and are happy to pay the 0.65% expense ratio for this endeavor. We are not the ones to argue with this view, especially when the CEF is correctly set up for persistent inflationary pressures.

Conclusion

PEO is a closed end fund. The name invests in energy and materials equities, and represents a robust way to extract dividends from the underlying asset class. The CEF has a covered distribution and stable NAV. With a persistent inflation picture PEO is set to continue to deliver net positive returns, thus an upgrade to 'Hold', even if using ETF building blocks is cheaper.

data.title: Retail Sales Up 0.6% In June, Better Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Census Bureau's Advance Retail Sales Report for June showed consumer spending was higher than expected last month, with headline sales rising 0.6%. This marks the first monthly increase since March and was better than the expected 0.1% rise.

Here is the introduction from today's report:

Advance estimates of U.S. retail and food services sales for June 2025, adjusted for seasonal variation and holiday and trading-day differences, but not for price changes, were $720.1 billion, up 0.6 percent (±0.5 percent) from the previous month, and up 3.9 percent (±0.5 percent) from June 2024. Total sales for the April 2025 through June 2025 period were up 4.1 percent (±0.4 percent) from the same period a year ago. The April 2025 to May 2025 percent change was unrevised from down 0.9 percent (±0.2 percent).Retail trade sales were up 0.6 percent (±0.5 percent) from May 2025, and up 3.5 percent (±0.5 percent) from last year. Nonstore retailers were up 4.5 percent (±1.4 percent) from last year, while food service and drinking places were up 6.6 percent (±1.8 percent) from June 2024.

The chart below is a log-scale snapshot of retail sales since the early 1990s. The three exponential regressions through the data help us to evaluate the long-term trend of this key economic indicator.

  1. The light purple line is a linear regression through the complete data series.
  2. The green line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2007 and then extrapolated to the present, thus excluding the Financial Crisis.
  3. The blue line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2019 and then extrapolated to the present, thus excluding the COVID-19 pandemic.

Monthly retail sales have been above the light purple and blue line since March 2021, signaling increased consumer spending that was most likely pent up as a result of the pandemic.

The year-over-year percent change provides another perspective on the historical trend. Current retail sales are up 3.9% compared to one year ago. Here is the headline series with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Core Retail Sales

Core sales (ex Autos) were up 0.5% in June. This is up from May's -0.2% reading and higher than the expected 0.3% growth in core sales.

Core retail sales are up 3.4% compared to one year ago. Here is the year-over-year chart of core retail sales with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Retail Sales: “Control” Purchases

The next two charts illustrate retail sales “control” purchases, which is an even more “core” view of retail sales. This series excludes motor vehicles & parts, gasoline, building materials, as well as food services & drinking places.

The popular financial press typically ignores this series, but it's a more consistent and reliable reading of the economy. Retail sales control purchases rose 0.5% in June. This is up from May's 0.2% reading and better than the expected 0.3% growth in control sales.

Similar to the retail sales snapshot chart earlier, the chart below is a log-scale snapshot of control purchases since the early 1990s and includes two of the exponential regressions previously mentioned.

Here is the same series year-over-year. Current control purchases are up 4.1% compared to one year ago.

For a better sense of the reduced volatility of the “control” series, here is a YoY overlay with the headline retail sales. Note that the two series follow each other closely, but headline sales have more extreme highs and lows than the control series.

Retail sales will impact interest in the SPDR S&P Retail ETF (XRT), VanEck Retail ETF (RTH), Amplify Online Retail ETF (IBUY), and ProShares Online Retail ETF (ONLN).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Deckers Outdoor: Robust Cash Generator With Growth Optionality
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Joon Jeon as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Deckers Outdoor has a largely dual business model with its UGG brand serving as a reliable cash cow and its HOKA brand providing most of the growth. The past two earnings announcements have brought into question the sustainability of HOKA’s continued growth. Management acknowledged the US direct-to-consumer (DTC) softness for HOKA, even though they reaffirmed their confidence in its long-term growth potential based on brand recognition in US and solid international momentum. Valuation has compressed significantly to ~15.7x forward earnings as investors re-evaluated the growth prospects of Deckers Outdoor, likely pricing in a significant near-term slowdown in HOKA’s growth.

However, it seems like the market is overlooking potential sources of upside surprise including HOKA’s healthy international momentum driven by increasing brand awareness, its ability to expand across different categories, and the product refresh cycle across HOKA’s flagship products. Deckers Outdoor offers an attractive entry point for patient investors seeking downside protection from strong balance sheet and consistent cash flow with growth optionality that is currently not priced into its share prices.

Business Overview

Deckers Outdoor operates a brand portfolio anchored by its two flagship brands, UGG and HOKA, that together make up roughly 95% of the revenue. The rest of the revenue comes from smaller brands such as Teva, AHNU, and Koolaburra. Deckers Outdoor distributes its products through both wholesale and DTC channels, following an omni-channel strategy. Wholesale still represents over 55% of revenue, but DTC has grown faster and carries higher gross margins. Since the onset of COVID, DTC revenue has grown at a 20% annualized rate, compared to 14% for wholesale. According to the latest management commentary, a balanced revenue mix will be achieved in the long term, with wholesale and DTC revenue each contributing approximately 50% of the revenue.

The UGG brand has generated consistent single-digit growth with exceptionally strong brand-level operational profitability while the HOKA brand has recorded a meteoric 47% annualized growth since 2019 while maintaining brand-level operational profitability on par with UGG in recent years. At the consolidated level, Deckers Outdoor has experienced accelerated revenue growth from mid-single digit growth pre-2021 to mid-teens growth post-2021, driven by the HOKA brand’s growth. What’s more remarkable, Deckers Outdoor has maintained an average operating margin of 20% during that period, well above competition.

UGG, which contributes approximately 50% of total revenue, is a mature, high-margin lifestyle brand known for its iconic sheepskin boots and enduring consumer loyalty. While its core product, the Classic Boot, remains the cornerstone of the brand, UGG has expanded its offerings across slippers and sneakers to increase its appeal across both seasons (365 wearability) and demographics (men’s focus illustrated by the company's Post Malone campaign). Since 2019, UGG has grown at a steady 9% annualized rate while maintaining an exceptionally attractive brand-level operating margin, serving as a dependable source of cash flow and supporting the growth of HOKA.

HOKA, accounting for roughly 45% of revenue according to the latest 10-K, is the company’s primary growth engine. Originally designed for ultrarunners, HOKA has redefined the performance footwear category with its maximalist cushioning and Meta-Rocker sole geometry. The brand has scaled rapidly, growing at a 47% annualized rate since 2019. Even more remarkably, HOKA has maintained superior profitability through its pull model that aligns production and distribution with consumer demand that reinforces the brand’s premium positioning through relative scarcity.

There are over five $100 million franchises (Bondi, Clifton, Mach, Arahi, and Skyflow) that carved out distinct niche and contributed significantly to the strong growth of HOKA. In terms of pricing, they are positioned at premium tier, significantly above the entry level models but below high-end level models offered by incumbents such as Nike and Adidas. Bondi represents HOKA’s most cushioned road shoe that appeals particularly to nurses and older consumers seeking comfort. Bondi 9, with even higher stack height, was introduced during Q3 of fiscal year 2025 and recorded positive wholesale feedback. Clifton, HOKA’s flagship model, combines cushioning with light weight for broad appeal across runners and lifestyle consumers. Clifton 10, its newest iteration, was launched in April 2025 and remains the biggest growth driver for fiscal year 2026. Mach is designed for serious runners who seek responsive shoes for race preparations. Arahi is geared towards over-pronators, providing support without the stiffness of traditional stability shoes. Arahi 8, its redesigned motion-control shoe, have just been launched in July 2025. Finally, Skyflow features traction and cushioning that are built for outdoor trail runners.

Recent Earnings and Market Reaction

Deckers’ fiscal Q3 2025 earnings report triggered a sharp 20% one-day selloff, driven by investor concerns over HOKA’s ability to sustain 20%+ growth amid signs of slowing U.S. DTC momentum. Management attributed the softness to inventory timing and model transitions but guided to a Q4 deceleration, which further rattled sentiment. The stock fell from $223.98 in late January to $100.88 by April, despite no material deterioration in long-term fundamentals.

Q4 results showed HOKA revenue of $495 million (+10% YoY), with gross margins holding steady at 56.7%. However, the absence of FY2026 guidance led to another 20% post-earnings decline. The stock is now down over 50% from its highs, reflecting a market that appears to be pricing in a significant slowdown in HOKA’s growth trajectory.

Valuation and DCF Walkthrough

A five-year sum-of-the-parts DCF valuation, adjusted for corporate overhead (14% of the revenue based on data between 2023 and 2025), shows that conservative revenue growth and margin assumptions seem to be implied in DECK’s current valuation. This valuation effort ignores the “other” segment that accounts for about 5% of revenue for simplicity and conservatism.

HOKA brand is the primary driver for the valuation of Deckers Outdoor and its success hinges on maintaining the healthy growth in the higher margin DTC channel. Back of the envelope approximation suggests that there is DTC margin premium of about 10% compared to wholesale margin for both UGG and HOKA. If HOKA grows at the projected rate across both DTC and wholesale channels, the revenue mix would reach about 50/50 split at the end of the 5-year projection. While the implied UGG revenue growth assumptions are reasonable based on recent history, the implied HOKA revenue growth assumptions seem to reflect a slowdown from the 47% annualized revenue growth since 2019.

UGG HOKA Corporate Overhead Deckers Outdoor
Tax Rate 22.0% 22.0% NA 22.0%
Depreciation 1.5% 1.5% Na 1.5%
CapEx + Working Capital 2.0% 2.0% NA 2.0%
WACC 9.0% 9.0% 9.0% 9.0%
Terminal Growth Rate 2.5% 2.5% 2.5% 2.5%
Revenue Growth Wholesale 8.0% 15.0% 11.9%
Revenue Growth DTC 8.0% 27.0% 17.1%
Wholesale Margin Estimate 30.0% 35.0% 33.0%
DTC Margin Estimate 40.0% 45.0% 43.0%
Corporate Overhead Margin 14.0%
NPV of Cash Flows $ 3,293.40 $ 4,545.48 $ 3,822.73 $ 4,016.15
PV of Terminal Value $ 7,214.16 $ 12,387.76 $ 9,431.64 $ 10,170.28
Total Valuation $ 10,507.57 $ 16,933.23 $ 13,254.37 $ 14,186.43
Shares Outstanding (MM) 149.40
Share Price $ 94.96

According to a couple market research reports, the global athletic footwear market is estimated to be between $150 billion and $180 billion in size for the 2024-2025 period. The three leading sportswear players, namely Nike, Inc. (NKE), Adidas AG (OTCQX:ADDYY), and Puma SE (PMMFY) all seem to derive the majority of their revenue from footwear sales – about 65%, 65%, and 55%, respectively. Based on 2025 revenues of the three companies generated about $30 billion, $15 billion, and $5 billion from footwear, making up about 27~33% of the global footwear revenue. This suggests the market remains reasonably fragmented with room for smaller players like HOKA to grow. Given the relatively low barrier to entry, brand differentiation and innovation can continue to carve out additional market share. At $2.2 billion of revenue, HOKA represented only about 1% of the total addressable market, highlighting its significant room for growth.

Room For Growth

First, raising brand awareness based on its unique product offering is critical in supporting HOKA’s growth potential. HOKA has established its brand as an innovator when it effectively created a new category when it popularized the concept of high-stack, lightweight cushioning that reduces fatigue and joint impact when the rest of the market was focused on slimmer, fashion-forward silhouettes. This innovative image for HOKA is an important asset and can continue to fuel further upside if they continue to increase brand awareness that remains relatively low at about 50% in the US and 30% internationally. In particular, international markets, which currently account for about 34% of HOKA’s revenue and grew at robust 39% YoY in 2025, can benefit from higher brand awareness.

Second, continued product innovation will be the key growth driver. New materials, foam enhancements, and new silhouettes such as the Skyward X, Skyflow, and Mafate Speed Lite, along with strategic expansion into lifestyle, fitness, and lateral movement categories should help HOKA regain its momentum. Recent launches, including flagship Bondi 9 and Clifton 10 have been well received by influencer and runner communities and should translate into a better earnings readout in the next few quarters.

Third, both UGG and HOKA demonstrated strong product discipline to maintain their industry leading margins. While the tariff-related cost pressures (and uncertainties) will likely be a headwind, their premium positioning should allow them to pass through a substantial amount of the increased costs to consumers. As long as they are careful to communicate sufficient value enhancements associated with price increases to consumers, both brands should be able to scale profitably as they have in the past.

Deckers Outdoor is generating robust cash flow, putting itself in a great position to pursue strategic acquisitions of promising brands for additional growth drivers. Even without brand acquisition, however, Deckers shares have more than a 30% upside at a target price of $127 if HOKA can regain its growth momentum and scale its revenue (assuming about 50/50 DTC and wholesale mix) to about half of Adidas 2025 revenue by the year 2030 as shown below.

UGG HOKA Corporate Overhead Deckers Outdoors
Revenue Growth Wholesale 8.0% 25.0% 18.1%
Revenue Growth DTC 8.0% 40.0% 24.9%
Wholesale Margin Estimate 30.0% 35.0% 33.5%
DTC Margin Estimate 40.0% 45.0% 43.5%
NPV of Cash Flows $ 3,293.40 $ 6,135.33 $ 4,545.18 $ 4,883.55
PV of Terminal Value $ 7,214.16 $ 19,561.38 $ 12,667.08 $ 14,108.46
Total Valuation $ 10,507.57 $ 25,696.71 $ 17,212.26 $ 18,992.01
Shares Outstanding (MM) 149.40
Share Price $ 127.12

Catalysts

Several catalysts could support a re-rating. In the near-term, product cycle momentum could restore investor confidence in HOKA’s popularity. The recent launches of Clifton 10 and Arahi 8 have been well-received by the influencer community and may help clarify the forward growth path. International expansion efforts, highlighted by the opening of a new flagship store in Shanghai, also presents an important growth driver where the near-term focus will be in the EMEA and Asia where brand awareness expansion has significant room. In addition, HOKA’s push into adjacent categories such as lifestyle, fitness, and recovery through introduction of new, slimmer silhouettes broadens its market beyond performance running and niche consumers such as healthcare professionals and elderly.

In the medium-term, capital allocation optionality supported by the $2.5 billion share buyback program (now nearly 20% of the market cap) represents a meaningful return of capital to shareholders. Deckers Outdoor enjoyed lofty valuations thanks to both its growth and profitability, and the recent compression creates opportunity for more value-conscious investors who were formerly disinclined to buy shares. With management having already removed formal guidance and the initial panic from uncertainty has been sufficiently baked into the share price, there is a solid case for a reset if management can provide more optimistic commentary or re-establish guidance to anchor investor expectations in the next few earnings cycles.

Risks and Mitigation

First, competitive pressure is intensifying in this highly competitive segment as incumbents like Nike, Adidas, and Puma are spending heavily on foam and materials technology and emerging brands like On Running are specializing around aesthetic appeal. Deckers can compete with the major players by consistent cash generation from UGG helping HOKA’s innovation initiatives to punch above its weight. In addition, the strategic shift into slimmer silhouette designs could broaden its appeal to everyday consumers and better compete with emerging brands.

Second, management expects a $150 million gross impact in fiscal year 2026. Not only are these costs being offset by supplier cost sharing and mix shift towards higher margin DTC business, continued disciplined brand management and pull model should help Deckers Outdoor stay ahead of the pack and sustain its industry-leading profit margins.

Finally, slower consumer spending from potential recession could impact all consumer discretionary categories. However, Decker’s premium positioning, loyal customer base, and pristine balance sheet will help them weather the storm.

Conclusion

Deckers is an undervalued opportunity with room to grow amidst fragmented competition, and strong brand recognition paired with good operating margins. And so, I rate it a Buy.

data.title: Digi Power X: A Tiny Stock With A Big Super Micro Bet
data.description:
Introduction

The July 17th move in Digi Power X Inc. (NASDAQ:DGXX) wasn’t just another microcap spike on vague promises. This time, the company placed an actual purchase order with Super Micro Computer (SMCI) for Nvidia (NVDA) B200 (Blackwell) systems, which will power their new AI platform called NeoCloud. The stock exploded over 30% in premarket and held on to a solid 7.5% gain by market close. And I think that reaction makes sense, because it’s not a future promise; it’s an actual, active move in the right direction.

About Digi Power X

DGXX is easy to dismiss because it sounds like a buzzword smoothie. It’s a small-cap stock with crypto exposure, modular pods, energy, and AI. But take a second look, and it starts to get more interesting. They are mining bitcoin, but it’s not as straightforward as you might think. They’re setting up Tier 3-level, AI-ready data infrastructure. Portable modular pods (ARMS 200s) that can be deployed without needing to build a hyperscale facility from scratch. That’s the idea behind their NeoCloud platform.

And even before all this AI talk, DGXX was already running a business. They’ve got about 100MW of active capacity spread across three locations, with plans to scale up to 200MW or more. They’re mining bitcoin, hosting third-party miners, reselling energy, stacking solar credits in New York, and considering battery storage in Buffalo (maybe they are doing more, but this is what I have found). They even have some cash flow from it.

Super Micro Deal

The first time you could hear about Super Micro and Digi Power X relations was back in May, something about their subsidiary working on a GPU rack deployment. At the time, the stock jumped 15% premarket on the news, but it wasn’t as important because this time it was a definitive order. Nvidia B200s are being installed into their modular pods, and we will most likely see the rollout already in the fourth quarter.

This is what changes things. For the first time, it feels like this company is determined to work hard and achieve its goals. The main goal, as you will see in the quote below, is to bring in Tier 3 AI compute. Besides, it’s a good sign they ordered the best to make it work—Blackwell B200s.

With Supermicro’s advanced B200 systems, we are now taking the steps to transition from infrastructure buildout to revenue generation. Our goal is to deliver Tier 3 AI-ready capacity equipped with the world’s most powerful GPUs for the generative AI era.

Crypto and Energy

Most of the money comes from crypto and energy. In May, DGXX brought in around $4.3 million from bitcoin mining and energy sales. They mined 35 BTC that month. By the end of June, the total crypto and cash balance jumped 45% in a month to $13.5 million.

Q2 energy revenue hit $2.3 million, and they’re stacking extra value from side projects like a solar site in New York and potential battery integration. These are things most people aren’t looking at when they glance at the stock, but it’s good because it means they’re not depending entirely on one future launch. They’ve already got multiple revenue streams, which, hopefully, will only grow over time.

Valuation

Look, I’m not going to sugarcoat this; the valuation is way ahead of the fundamentals. P/B close to 5x, P/S around 3x, no earnings, negative margins—if you’re looking for a safe entry, this isn’t it. But sometimes valuation doesn’t tell the whole story, especially with early-stage companies chasing multiple fast-growing markets.

The Super Micro deal makes it more interesting because there is an actual deployment scheduled. And there’s already revenue from bitcoin and from energy.

Besides the valuation, I noticed that ownership is mostly in the hands of individual investors (about 67% + almost 14% by individuals/insiders). That kind of ownership structure creates a very specific type of market behavior, like we saw today. Volatility on any sentiment and headline, and not necessarily on fundamentals.

If you’ve followed enough smallcaps before, you know how this works. When a company with a retail-heavy float announces something big (very much like a deal with Super Micro) or anything with Nvidia’s name in it, the stock doesn’t just go up 2–3%; it can fly 30%. Hello, DGXX, today on the premarket.

But it works both ways. That same setup makes the stock fragile. If the company delays a product launch, misses a target, or if the investors get nervous for whatever reason, you’ll see DGXX fall just as fast as it climbed, if not faster. There’s no cushion of long-term institutional holders who’d be expected to keep holding. It’s a news-driven, momentum stock, and that kind of ownership base is like jet fuel when things go right and a wrecking ball when they don’t.

That said, I also looked at their balance sheet, and while it’s far from perfect, they’re not buried under debt. There’s some cash to support near-term operations, and they’re clearly trying to keep the dilution risk low for now. All of this makes DGXX the kind of stock you have to watch like a hawk, not something you set and forget. But if they deliver a few more solid updates, the upside could be explosive. You just have to know what you're getting into.

Biggest Red Flags

They’re still losing a lot of money, and gross margins are low. Almost every profitability metric is in the red. That’s not unusual for early-stage data infrastructure or mining companies, but it’s still a problem. If revenue doesn’t scale up quickly, they’ll need to raise more cash. And that would almost certainly mean dilution.

Also, the bitcoin (BTC-USD) side of the business, which is one of the main revenue drivers, depends heavily on BTC prices. If crypto has a rough few quarters, its cash will disappear very fast.

Conclusion

DGXX is messy. But it’s the kind of messy that might become something really interesting. The AI dream is on the way, which, I must say, is great. The company has already gone further than most microcap stocks in this space. The crypto and energy business is carrying the company while that happens. And for a company this small, it looks like a solid setup.

Now pay attention; it’s solid, but that does not guarantee the success of any kind. If it makes this whole hybrid AI-crypto-energy thing work, this stock won’t stay at these levels for long, and as I already said, it has the potential to fly sky-high.

So, at this point, the decision is all yours, but if I were investing in it, I would treat it as a call option. Either it surges and makes you rich, or forget about all the money you have put in. Most likely, you won’t get it back.

data.title: Domino's Pizza: All Priced In, No Mistakes Allowed
data.description:
Flash Investment Thesis

Domino’s Pizza (NASDAQ:DPZ) is one of the most efficient companies in the restaurant sector. Its franchise model, operating margins above 18%, and ROIC exceeding 30% have turned it into a compounding machine for more than a decade.

However, at current levels —with a share price of $464, a forward P/E of 26x, and a FCF yield around 4.1%— the market is already pricing in most of that story. Margins remain solid, but the pace of net store openings is decelerating, delivery has lost traction in the U.S., and international expansion doesn't seem strong enough to sustain a valuation premium.

With a PEG ratio above 3x, the market is paying more than three dollars for every dollar of expected growth. That means Domino’s Pizza needs to surprise again.

My base case values the stock at $455 —slightly below the current price. It’s a reasonable level for those seeking stability and disciplined returns, but not attractive enough to initiate a new position.

Only if the price pulls back toward the $430–435 range —where the forward P/E drops to 24x and the FCF yield approaches 4.2%— would the opportunity start to make sense.

Domino’s Pizza remains a great business. But at this price, I rate it as a Hold for those already in the position.

Overview

Domino’s Pizza is not a traditional restaurant chain. 98% of its more than 20,000 stores are operated by franchisees. The company doesn’t rely on selling pizzas directly, but on three core revenue streams: brand royalties, ingredient sales through its supply chain, and logistics commissions.More than a fast-food chain, it operates as a service platform for franchisees.Unlike competitors such as Texas Roadhouse (TXRH) or Dutch Bros (BROS), which have high operating costs, Domino’s Pizza outsources almost all of the risk. Its CapEx represents just 2.3% of sales, while return on capital exceeds 32%.

Since 2011, Domino’s Pizza has tripled its global footprint, and in the last 12 months alone, it has added more than 1,200 net new stores.

Nearly 60% of net store growth now comes from outside the U.S. And while that provides some room—given the potential in LATAM, Africa, and parts of Asia—it doesn’t fully offset the maturity of the domestic market, where growth has stalled for several quarters.Operationally, Domino’s stands out more for profitability than scale. Its 18.56% operating margin is the highest in its peer group, ahead of Yum China Holdings, Inc. (YUMC) with 10.87%, and Luckin Coffee Inc. (OTCPK:LKNCY) with 11.49%, respectively.

Even though it remains the most profitable operator in its category, the thesis is starting to lose ground to newer models. Companies like Luckin Coffee and Dutch Bros. Inc (BROS) are growing faster, with a lighter structures and less leverage. Domino’s Pizza, by contrast, carries over $4.9B in net debt and a Net Debt/EBITDA ratio above 5x, which limits its strategic flexibility.

The market no longer rewards efficiency alone. And when a company trades at 26x earnings, that expectation becomes unavoidable.

Is Every Dollar Still Worth It?

Domino’s Pizza currently trades at $464 — a price that already reflects much of its historical narrative. With a forward P/E close to 26x and a free cash flow yield of around 4.1%, there’s no obvious undervaluation here.

The company has historically commanded a premium multiple —and for good reason. Its ROIC exceeds 30%, operating margins are consistently above 18%, and shareholder returns are solid and predictable. Over the last three years, the dividend has grown at a 17.8% CAGR, and share buybacks have reduced the outstanding count by over 15%. It’s a predictable cash-generating machine —but one increasingly driven by efficiency rather than growth.

But, at these levels, the upside appears exhausted. With estimated EPS of $18 in 2026, applying a base multiple of 25x leads to a fair value of around $455 in two years —below the current price.

Even in a bullish scenario —assuming multiple expansion to 30x— the upside target would rise to $546, implying just an +8.5% annualized return from today’s price.

Conversely, in a more conservative scenario using a 22x multiple, the target drops to the $400–405 range —a potential downside of -12% to -14%.

The market expects Domino’s Pizza to keep outperforming in order to justify this premium. But with moderate organic growth, stagnant U.S. delivery sales, and no clear short-term catalysts, that bar is getting harder to clear.

In this context, Domino’s Pizza is a hold. It makes sense to keep the stock if already in the portfolio, but the risk-reward profile doesn’t justify new entries.

Only if the price corrects to the $430–435 range —where the FCF yield rises above 4.2%, the forward multiple falls closer to 24x, and technical support gains traction— would a tactical entry start to make sense.

The Price of Stability

Let’s start with the obvious, Domino’s isn’t in trouble. Its model is still working. But at these multiples, there’s little margin of safety.And that’s where the problem begins.

To justify a forward P/E above 25x, the company needs to grow earnings by around 10% annually. That’s the roadmap analysts are pricing in. The issue is that fundamentals are out of sync, and here’s why: Organic growth has been losing steam for several quarters, and while international expansion still contributes, it’s unclear whether it will be enough.

Then there’s the leverage. Domino’s has been aggressively buying back shares using debt, which now exceeds $5.2 billion, compared to its having just $300 million in cash. Its Net Debt/EBITDA ratio is over 5x. So as long as margins hold and store openings continue, it’s manageable. But the room to maneuver is tight, and in a high-rate environment, that’s a heavy backpack to carry.

The last point is saturation. The company continues to open stores at a healthy pace, but its growth rate has slowed from 8.2% in 2021 to 5.6% in 2024. If the market stops rewarding expansion for its own sake, one of the thesis’s key pillars starts to wobble.

Domino’s is still a great company. But the bar is high — and even a slight disappointment could prompt the market to re-rate the stock.

Conclusion

My conclusion is to maintain a hold rating on Domino’s Pizza.

Why? Because the company is still in good shape. Margins are stable, cash flow is strong, and the franchise model remains efficient. But at $464 per share, the market is already pricing in that everything goes right.

The EPS estimate for 2026 is $18. Assigning a reasonable multiple of 25x, the price target lands around $455—slightly below current levels. That multiple is justified by historical valuation ranges and by what’s reasonable given the company’s profile and sector.

In an optimistic scenario, with multiple expansion to 30x, the stock could reach $546. But that would require everything to click. Stronger margins, growth in key markets, maybe even an unexpected catalyst. Not impossible, but not the base case either.

On the flip side, if the market adjusts the multiple to 22x, the price would fall toward $405—a 12% drop from current levels.

With that range on the table, the upside doesn’t justify new entries. The company is delivering. Now we’ll see if the market still thinks that’s enough.

data.title: Ares Management: Rapid Growth In Alternative Investments
data.description:
I rate Ares Management Corporation (NYSE:ARES) a Hold for capital appreciation-focused investors who are looking for long-term buy-and-hold investments. The stock went public in 2014 and has been on an incredible tear to record highs and appears to be overbought. The California-based alternative asset manager is known for its high-caliber, institution-quality investments, especially in private credit.

Ares is the parent of the business development company (BDC) Ares Capital Corporation (NASDAQ:ARCC), which focuses on direct lending, Ares Commercial Real Estate Corporation (NYSE:ACRE), and Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (NYSE:ARDC).

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are:

  • Pays a rising quarterly dividend with a 2.49% annual yield
  • Trades at a significant premium P/E ratio to peers
  • Strong annual growth in AUM and direct investors, which has led to 18% annual growth in management fee revenues over the past 12+ years
  • Expanded investor strategies with larger, scaled funds
  • Significant opportunities for global expansion
  • Industry consolidation favors larger asset managers with deep resources

Full disclosure: I own shares in ARCC and ARDC for the income generation. Within the BDC/credit space, ARCC has earned a reputation as one of the top-tier, blue-chip companies with record-setting total returns.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Hold 3.25 (as of 7/17/2025), which supports my rating. Note the valuation of an “F” due to the metrics showing a premium.

High-Quality Alternative Asset Manager

Ares Management Corporation is a leading global alternative investment manager offering clients complementary primary and secondary investment solutions across the credit, real estate, private equity, and infrastructure asset classes.

Founded in 1997, it is headquartered in Los Angeles, California, with offices in Europe and Asia. The company has assets under management (AUM) of $546 billion, with 4,100 employees in over 50 global offices.

As shown in the chart below, Ares' largest business segment is credit with $359.1 billion in AUM, followed by real assets at $124.2 billion.

Within each of these business segments, Ares points out that their share of the total addressable market, or TAM, is tiny, although they are leaders. The conclusion is that they have much more runway to build the business. For example, if the credit addressable market is $40 trillion and Ares has $359 billion of it, that equates to a 0.9% share of the market.

Combining the total addressable markets of Credit, Real Estate, Infrastructure, Private Equity, and Secondaries is equivalent to $90 trillion. Ares has an AUM of $546 billion; therefore, their share is only 0.6%.

Q1 2025 Earnings Results

On May 5, the company declared Q1 earnings, with the following financial highlights:

  • Grew total revenues of $1.09 billion by 54%, but expenses grew at a higher rate, leading to a drop in net income by 40%.
  • Net income attributable to Ares was $0.00 for the quarter, compared to $0.33 in the prior year period.
  • Fee-related earnings grew 22% to $367.3 million.
  • Realized income grew 40% to $405.9 million.
  • AUM grew 27% to $545.9 billion.
  • Perpetual capital grew 42% to $154.5 billion.

Overall, the quarter showed growth in AUM and fee-related earnings, which increased revenue; however, expenses were higher, absorbing net income. In my experience, growth can be a challenge, where a company pays to grow by hiring people and investing in the business, which creates short-term declines in profitability.

Superior Performance to Date

Ares has experienced strong annual growth in AUM and increased the number of direct institutional investors they work with, which has led to 18% annualized growth of management fee revenue over the past 12 years.

This extraordinary growth in fee revenue has translated into index-beating share price growth since going public.

An investment of $10,000 over the past 10 years in ARES would have grown to $144,930 today, far outperforming the S&P 500 (SPY) at $34,910 and the Financial Services Sector (XLF) at $30,500.

Premium Priced Valuation

Ares has a high price-to-earnings ratio vs. the sector, which drives Seeking Alpha Quant’s valuation grade of F. For example, Ares P/E on trailing twelve months is 102.35 vs. 13.61 for the sector median. On a forward basis, Ares has a P/E of 35 vs. the sector median of 11.

While it’s normal to have the sector leader priced at a premium, it appears the valuations are at a super premium, creating a risk for the elevated stock value to drop during times of stress. Similar to tech stocks, in my view, Ares may be driven by emotional enthusiasm behind the extraordinary opportunity, but will lose its luster during a retreat driven by the next fear-inducing correction.

Using another metric, price to book, Ares forward metric is 8.8 vs. the sector median at 1.16, a 643% premium. Once again, while the company is worth a premium vs. the sector, the 759%+ premium appears to be too high.

In looking at a chart of the dividend growth over the past 10 years, we can see how investors are enthusiastic about the stock. From 2015 to the present, the rise in dividends makes a symmetrical arc upward, following the growth in fee-related revenues.

In the chart below, we can see the actual distributions per share and have a more specific understanding of the value that is being built. For example, one share of Ares stock in 2015 paid $0.88 in distributions through the year. That same share of stock earned $3.72 for the year 2024, a 422.7% rise in just less than 10 years.

In comparison to peers, ARES has the strongest 5-year total return of 431%. The stock price is also premium priced, with the second highest price-to-book ratio of 13.1 and price earnings ratio of 35.2.

Blackstone Inc. (BX) is the largest alternative asset manager as ranked by market capitalization ($203B). Its stock is priced at a premium with a price-to-book ratio of 16.1 and a relatively higher P/E ratio of 33.4. Similar to all the asset managers, its 5-year total return is high at 272%.

KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) barely distributes a dividend, but rather is growing capital appreciation, with a 5-year total return of 348%, the second highest behind ARES.

Brookfield Asset Management Ltd. (BAM) distributes the second-highest dividend at 3.0%.

Apollo Global Management, Inc. (APO) delivers a strong 5-year total return of 246% but has a lower price-to-book ratio of 5.3.

Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) pays the highest dividend of the group at 4.5%, with the highest dividend growth rate and a lower price-to-book ratio, indicating it may be one of the better buys within the group.

Outlook

Ares Management has strong momentum going forward based on its quality business model and the projected growth of alternative investments. Most investors remain meaningfully underallocated to alternatives and are looking for trusted providers who can offer a broad array of product choices and can handle the scale needed.

Ares has grown AUM and its product offerings, which will lead to fee-related income increases in the future.

The next earnings report, for Q2, will be available pre-market on August 1, 2025.

Risks To Rating

ARES earnings tend not to be a smooth line. The income and earnings go up and down depending on the quarter. That means the stock is always at risk of not meeting Wall Street’s expectations. While it is expected that ARES will continue to grow, the associated expenses might overshadow income in the short term.

Bottom Line

ARES stock has had a tremendous run in the past 5 years, leading to a premium price; therefore, I rate the stock a Hold. Going into Q2, ARES has plenty of momentum to meet or exceed the earnings estimates based on the strength of the U.S. economy and lack of obstacles in its way.

ARES has grown AUM and client base, which should lead to growth in fee-related income and investment income. As companies in the alternative investment space tend to consolidate, ARES will take advantage of its size, make acquisitions, and become stronger overall.
